data.title: Check Point: The Outlook For An Elite Firewall
data.description:
Just a few days ago, Check Point Software (CHKP) filed its 20F with the SEC, and although we already knew the figures since February 12, I always like to review the report in detail to find or connect different pieces of the puzzle to answer the questions I ask myself.

Nadav Zafrir is completing his first year as CEO, so it is already becoming a sufficient timeframe to evaluate the outcome of the direction he wanted to take. Specifically, he promised to accelerate the subscriptions business, contain margins, and return to robust new customer acquisition with a focus on the US.

The result? He meets the first commitment with 11.5% growth in subscriptions, and misses on operating margin by three times what was anticipated, and what carries more weight for me, which is new customer acquisition, we have a total of 20,000 net new customers in email security. However, in SASE two thirds of growth comes from upsell to the existing firewall base, not from new customers. For me, he delivers on the basics, but if we take into account that markets price in expectations, it does not seem that these are enough.

The engine is losing pressure

CHKP’s core business is enterprise firewall, and still today it represents, according to my estimate reviewing the accounting lines, around 72% of revenue , so any analysis has to start and place this vertical at the center. A global market with a size of around $12B or $13B, with a projected double-digit growth through 2030, so taking into account that the company’s annual growth hovers around 6%, we may have the first clue behind the market’s discount with that growth gap.

If we now look at market share to check whether this is common across the sector, I will start by naming the main players, such as the clear leader, which is Palo Alto (PANW) with approximately 28% market share, practically doubling the next competitors Cisco (CSCO) and Fortinet (FTNT), which each hold around 15%. It is in fourth position where CHKP appears, with a share of around 8% to 10%.

With all the pieces on the board, I can say that the dynamic of PANW and FTN compared to CHKP is moving at a different speed. PANW, for example, is growing sales in its latest quarter by around 15%, with product revenue growing at 22%. FTN, on the other hand, was growing at around 16%, mainly driven by its secure networking hardware vertical, that is, firewall. This differs when compared to CHKP’s 6% revenue growth, with product revenue at 7.9%.

So the question is inevitable, if we are in the same market, with the same cycle, why as investors should we choose CHKP over its competitors?

First, there is a firewall renewal cycle that usually ranges between 3 and 5 years, where windows are created for the customer to switch vendor. Now, in 2025 we are seeing an increasing number of these proposals due to the incorporation of cloud firewall, which is logical considering the rise of AI, so now we have a decision that is starting to become more about architecture than a purely technical renewal.

PANW, for example, is betting on a strategy where it offers zero-cost periods so that customers can try its full platform before their contracts expire, in order to capture customers during these windows. This is very interesting, since through this approach, the customer has the option to test the entire value proposition, and the more pillars they end up contracting, the higher the switching cost later. This completes a strategy that incentivizes PANW to subsidize this entry cost, in exchange for future profitability through later consolidation.

CHKP also has this strategy in place with “Infinity Platform,” leveraging around 100,000 enterprise customers to carry out cross-selling, although with a relevant difference in the starting point, since in PANW’s case it has already been executing this strategy for several years, with metrics that I can quantify with ARR of $6.3B and RPO of $16B. If I add to that the price increases in 2026, and although it makes sense due to the global increase in memory chip costs, for me it is more a move to protect margins on the existing base rather than a strategy to compete on price for new customers.

So I think we are facing a competitive advantage that is currently being defended, rather than in a growth phase, in a market where renewal windows are becoming architectural decisions, and where I believe a reactive position will not be enough if they want to keep up with the competition.

A 6% that shines within a portfolio

If before I was talking about the loss of pressure in the main engine, now it is time to assess the health of the rest of the revenue mix, and I will start by talking about Avanan. For me, it is the only piece of the current portfolio that shows strong performance, essentially the current crown jewel.

Acquired in 2021 for around $300M, it had about 5,000 customers and an estimated ARR of around $10M to $20M, and today it has 65,000 customers and $160M in ARR, along with growth above 40% YoY. These are figures that, in my opinion, represent one of the best recent outcomes in terms of integration within cybersecurity, and in fact, CHKP was named a leader in Gartner for Email Security Platforms alongside Microsoft (MSFT) and Proofpoint.

Going deeper into its technical differentiation, most email security solutions work by redirecting traffic through their own servers, which requires changing the MX records of the client’s domain, that is, modifying the instructions that tell the internet where to route a company’s email. This is usually a complex task.

And this is where Avanan’s value proposition comes into play, as it connects directly via API to M365 without having to touch network configuration, which simplifies deployment, thus removing adoption friction for the customer. In addition to this, it also has an AI model that is specifically trained on attacks that manage to bypass Microsoft Defender’s native filters.

For me, this translates into an additional layer on top of M365, rather than a direct alternative, so customers that already use MSFT ecosystems will face less resistance when adopting this model.

So we have that Avanan is proof that CHKP can execute well when it bets on the right product. The problem is that with $160M in ARR over $2.7B in revenue, it practically represents 6% of the business, and it is too small to offset the loss of momentum in firewall.

And this is precisely what I look for in CHKP, that is, if they have been able to take a company from 5,000 customers to 65,000 in four years, they have the potential to do the same with the rest of the product portfolio. In addition, the starting point with around 100,000 enterprise customers is sufficient to drive cross-sell towards its main pillars. To this we must add $4.3B in cash and $2B in convertible notes, meaning they can execute another acquisition like they did with Avanan.

But to begin with, until SASE ARR exceeds $200M with clear evidence of net new enterprise customers, together with a shift in management’s narrative from “defense” to “attack,” these are the positive signals I look for to justify a change in trend.

Market expectations signal danger

Clearly, price does not solve the fundamental issues that CHKP seems to have, and it appears to be pricing in a scenario where the company’s problems will continue to worsen.

In the long-term time series, price seems to be returning to the area where the move that took it to the $230 highs in early 2025 began. From there, the sequence has been one of weakness, with higher volume in 2026. In addition to this, I also observe three bands in the volume profile that I interpret as three areas where there is a large volume of sellers between $210–$225, $177–$195 and $150–$166, which indicate that those participants now have unrealized losses, and when price recovers those levels, some pressure may be created again as these participants exit the stock.

Although everything seems to indicate, given the market situation, my main scenario is a return to an area between $120 and $125, where price should start to stabilize.

Today, at current prices, for the fundamental investor it makes more sense to build a position in the $125 area if a consolidation phase is confirmed, and not a continuation of the current bearish structure.

What do we do with CHKP?

To recap, I will take into account the company’s $2.7B in revenue, along with cash generation that remains resilient, but is growing at almost half the pace of the sector.

Firewall, representing around 72% of the revenue mix, is where CHKP’s intentions appear to be more defensive than focused on gaining share. Although for me, the positive point lies in Avanan, with current growth of 40% and only the “but” of representing just 6% of revenue and not being able to move the needle on its own.

Price seems to already reflect this operational deterioration mentioned, and is starting to approach relevant levels where it should stabilize. But technically, I still do not see signals of that, so my scenario, to which I assign the highest probability, points to a level between $120 and $125 before considering any buying scenario.

For these reasons, I prefer to assign a sell rating and review this position if there is evidence from CHKP of an acceleration in new customer acquisition (not upsell on the firewall base), along with a signal of price stabilization in the $120-$125 area. Until then, I prefer to stay on the sidelines and keep listening to the market.

data.title: Risk Of Escalation Discourages Risk Taking While U.S. Jobs Report May Extend The Dollar's Narrow Ranges
data.description:
The shroud of the Middle East war overhangs the Good Friday holiday. The attack on an Iranian bridge yesterday threatens a response in kind by Tehran, and this will serve to keep risk appetites in check ahead of the weekend. Many centers will be closed on Monday as well. The main interest today is with the US March jobs data. Because the labor force is barely growing, in good part due to the crackdown on immigration (both legal and illegal), various estimates suggest that practically no job growth is consistent with the new equilibrium. The US has created about 156k jobs over the past 12 months, and the unemployment rate has averaged 4.3%. The unemployment rate is at 4.4%, and that is the median projection for the end of the year by the Federal Reserve last month. The low level of unemployment seems to belie claims of stagflation.

Meanwhile, news that the US Army Chief of Staff General and two other generals were purged is being discussed widely. It seems unusual in the middle of a military conflict. Reports suggest clashes over promotions and operational strategy in Iran. While it could impact command continuity, it is arguably about aligning leadership with the administration’s goals. At the same time, it fans some fears that the US will put “boots on the ground”. This feeds into the market’s reluctance to take more risk. At the same time, the week ending Wednesday was the first in six weeks that foreign central banks did not draw on their custody holdings (Treasuries and Agencies) at the Federal Reserve. In fact, their holdings increased by almost $3.3 bln.

Prices

G10

• The euro’s sell-off after President Trump’s national address on Wednesday evening in the US saw it retrace (61.8%) of the week’s rally, slightly below $1.1515. It recovered to about $1.1565 before running out of steam. The thin markets have confined it to about $1.1530-$1.1545 so far today, trading as it were between two option strikes ($1.1525 for ~512 mln euros and $1.1550 for ~850 mln euros) that expire today. The US jobs data may expand the range, but the risk of escalation in the Middle East may deter strong gains ahead of the weekend.

• The dollar approached JPY159.75 in early North American turnover yesterday. It pulled back to around JPY159.25 before it recovered. It is trading quietly between about JPY159.45 and JPY159.70. Options for about $430 mln, struck at JPY160 expire today.

• Sterling recorded the low for the year on Tuesday near $1.3160. It rallied to around $1.3345 on Wednesday but retreated deeply back to almost $1.3180 yesterday. It recovered to about $1.3250 before stalling. Sterling is in a narrow range of about $1.3220 to $1.3245. Options for GBP375 mln at $1.32 expire today.

• The US dollar (DXY) recorded the year’s high against the Canadian dollar on Tuesday near CAD1.3965. It backed off to almost CAD1.3870 on Wednesday and caught a new bid yesterday that lifted it to CAD1.3935 yesterday. It is confined to about CAD1.3915 to slightly more than CAD1.3925. The daily momentum indicators are stretched, but they won’t prevent a challenge on this week’s high and possibly a test of the CAD1.4000-15 area.

• The Australian dollar was sold to $0.6860 in the risk-off moves that followed President Trump’s address. It recovered to almost $0.6920 in North American turnover but has remained slightly below there today. It frayed $0.6900, but not by much, and options for A$840 mln at $0.6880 expire today, as do ~A$540 mln options struck at $0.6870.

EM

• The dollar recorded an inside day against the Mexican peso yesterday, having remained in Wednesday’s range (~MXN17.7960-MXN17.9680). The local markets were closed yesterday and remain on holiday today. It has been confined to a narrow range around yesterday’s MXN17.87 close, with a small downside bias.

• The dollar found support against the offshore yuan in front of CNH6.8720. It has not traded below CNH6.8700 for three weeks. Although the dollar traded up to CNH6.9040, it settled below CNH6.89. It has hardly spent any time above yesterday’s settlement and slipped slightly below CNH6.88. The PBOC set the dollar’s reference rate sharply lower in the past two sessions (by a total of a little less than 0.5%) and lowered it for the third consecutive session yesterday. It has not been set lower for four consecutive sessions this year. The pattern held, and today’s fix was set at CNY6.8929 (CNY6.8880 yesterday, a new three-year low, and CNY6.9141 a week ago).

• Indian markets are closed for the holiday today.

Other Markets

• The holiday has seen many financial centers close. The equity markets in the Asia Pacific region that were open were mixed. The Nikkei gained about 1.25% and South Korea’s Kospi gained 2.75%. The others, including mainland China and several smaller bourses, eased. European bourses are closed, leaving the Stoxx 600 (STOXX) with about a 2.7% gain this week. The US stock market is closed today, but the futures are off by around 0.2%-0.3%. They are up 2.6%-3.5% on the week ahead of the US jobs report.

• Benchmark 10-year yields are flat. The 10-year JGB yield slipped slightly and is up about 1.5 bp this week. European bond yields are off 4-13 bp lower this week, and peripheral premiums narrowed. The US 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) is at 4.30%, which is about 4.5 bp lower than it settled last week.

• The holiday means that gold, silver and oil are not trading.

Data

• The median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey is for a 65k rise in March US non-farm payrolls, and the unemployment rate is seen unchanged at 4.4%. Of course, the February loss of 92k jobs is subject to revision. Since last April’s Liberation Day, the US has lost about 20k jobs overall and 90k in the manufacturing sector. Part of the reason for the persistent revisions is that businesses appear more reluctant to participate in a timely fashion. The final services and composite PMI are of passing interest as the preliminary estimate is “close enough”.

• Despite the Good Friday holiday, France reported a larger-than-expected 0.7% decline in February industrial output and January’s 0.5% increase was revised to 0.2%. The aggregate estimate for the eurozone is due April 15.

• Japan reported its final March services and composite PMI. The former stands at 53.4, up from the initial estimate of 52.8 but down from 53.8 in February, its highest level since April 2024. The composite is at 53.0, better than the 52.5 flash estimate but down from 53.9 in February.

• China’s RatingDog March services PMI slowed to 52.1 from 56.7. This unwinds February’s gain from 52.3 in January. The composite eased to 51.5 from 55.4. It averaged 51.3 in 2025 and 51.4 in Q4 25. The Q1 26 average is 52.8.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: I'm Building Income With The Most Essential Businesses In The World
data.description:
Introduction (A History & Supply Chain Lesson)

Investing during times of war is tough, as we are bombarded with daily headlines that make it nearly impossible to estimate how long it will take and how bad it will get. By the time I’m done writing this article, the market could be 5% lower or 5% higher, depending on what kind of news has hit the wire.

And, to be honest, as annoying as it may be, I’m writing this from a safe office, far away from any rocket impacts, which means my complaints are truly only about my new workload. Unlike some of my friends, I don’t have to be close to a bomb shelter.

The reason I’m telling you all of this is that I spent the past few days thinking about how this war, like almost any other war, is massively impacting supply chains and showing the world just how important prudent supply management is. While I’m painting with a broad brush, I think it’s fair to say that almost any major war in history was either about supply chains or decided about supply chains, or both.

Personally, I love World War II history, especially when it’s explained by people like Victor Davis Hanson, who does a great job of highlighting the mechanics behind the war machinery. For example, Japan moved into the Dutch East Indies in World War II to secure oil and rubber. Some say that if they hadn’t dragged the U.S. into the war by bombing Pearl Harbor, they would have been in a great (much better) spot to gain more territory and keep it. Once the U.S. got involved, Japan was quickly overwhelmed by its massive industrial power. Unlike nations like Germany, the U.S. was able to boost military output without having its factories bombed.

That’s also why Iwo Jima was so important. Although Japan was almost defeated, and Iwo Jima is one of the smallest islands in the area, it was critical for aircraft. By capturing it, the U.S. could fly much bigger bombing missions in Japan.

And then there’s Germany, which was forced to split its troops in 1942 during its Soviet Union invasion. They had to capture Russia’s Baku oil fields. It would both cut off Russian supply and provide German forces with enough fuel to continue. They failed.

Fast-forward to 2026, and it seems that every time a new conflict happens, the market finds out just how critical supply chains are and that while software and tech companies may have been the biggest growth drivers in recent years, when things get bad, it’s all about food and energy security. This is what the spike in fertilizer prices looked like:

In a recent article, a reader who you may know as “Geeky Mechanic” wrote for Main Street Alpha (my service comes to Seeking Alpha on April 7) about how critical this situation has become and why the U.S. is a standout here:

In the quest for food security, I think we will see an increasing number of trade deals and investments globally around the needed commodities, whether fertilizers or directly concerning specific agricultural goods. The U.S. stands to benefit in the long term as investments ramp up in the sector and global supply chains move to prevent any future disruptions. Other countries will also look to whoever can supply them to buy either fertilizers or finished agricultural goods. U.S. agricultural exports are already valued at $120 billion in yearly exports, which I think will continue its upward trend, and might even accelerate. - Main Street Alpha (Geeky Mechanic).

Moreover, because the Strait of Hormuz ships roughly a fifth of all seaborne oil and a quarter of global LNG, these commodities suddenly became scarce, which led to full-blown supply shortages in markets like Southeast Asia.

And although the U.S. also saw weakness in stock prices, declining investor sentiment, and higher recession odds, I think it’s more than fair to make the case that it has the resources to gain a competitive advantage. I discussed this in an article a few years ago titled “Red, White, And Wealth: Building An All-American Dividend Portfolio.”

• America has access to highly valuable energy commodities and minerals.• America has sufficient freshwater and fertile ground.• America has the world’s largest defense force to protect all of it.

I’m painting with a broad brush, but I think you get the idea.

Every time a conflict happens (think of the Ukraine and the Iran War) or a major disruption like the pandemic, the market is, once again, confronted by the harsh reality that our economies truly depend on physical supply chains. We learn that farmers don’t magically grow crops, that gasoline depends on complex networks, and that even things like plastic and helium are extremely critical.

That’s what this article is about, as I’ll highlight a number of terrific companies with critical operations in some of the world’s most important supply chains. Not only are these companies important right now, but I think a diversified basket of these stocks will generate elevated long-term returns. Even better, they come with either dividend growth or income.

So, as we have a lot more to discuss, let’s dive into the second part of this article.

The "Fortress Portfolio" - Critical Supply Chain Dividend Stocks

I don’t like event-driven investing. That’s why I don’t buy energy stocks just to benefit from a war. I owned these stocks before they happened. It’s for the same reason I don't always cancel my insurance the moment I expect risks of something bad happening are low.

The thing is that I want to own high-quality stocks that both shield me against mayhem and allow me to build wealth over time. Everything else doesn’t work for me, as my goal is to beat the market over time. That won’t work when I’m knee-deep into stocks that only perform well when global geopolitical risks explode. Can you imagine how poor my performance would be over time?

• Energy & Midstream

This one is the most obvious in light of what’s going on in Iran and the Middle East, in general. America has the Permian Basin, while Canada has the Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin. Both areas have deep reserves, low breakeven prices, and they are shielded against rocket strikes and other things that currently hurt OPEC’s output.

In this area, I want to be “always” long the most efficient players, including Diamondback Energy (FANG), Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), larger players like Exxon Mobil (XOM), and royalty-focused players like Viper Energy (VNOM). This basket has a variable dividend with a yield of roughly 5-6% at current oil prices (that’s back-of-the-napkin math).

Moreover, as I know that some readers dislike the dependence on oil prices, midstream companies are also a great way to buy income and safety, as these players own the critical infrastructure needed to process and ship the commodities produced by the aforementioned companies. Think of ONEOK (OKE), Kinder Morgan (KMI), Antero Midstream (AM), TC Energy (TRP), and Enbridge (ENB). All of these stocks are C-Corps that do not issue K-1 forms. If you don’t mind K-1s (or even like them), my favorites are Western Midstream (WES) and MPLX (MPLX). This basket should pay investors roughly 5-7% without elevated risks.

Essentially, the energy stocks I just gave you (upstream and midstream) give you perfect coverage of the entire North American energy industry if you were to buy all of them.

• Agriculture

Agriculture is a tough topic. On the one hand, it’s ultra critical, as we all need to eat. It’s even more important than housing, as even the homeless need to eat. On the other hand, agricultural goods, in general, tend to be very abundant. Fertilizer, for example, is a commodity group that mostly consists of potash, phosphates, and nitrogen. These are very abundant in the world, which means companies that produce these goods often rely on their own ability to produce more cheaply than others and supply/pricing shocks to grow.

In general, the industry tends to run on thin margins, as the overview below shows. Crop prices usually barely trade above total input costs, as it’s such a homogenous market.

One example is Mosaic (MOS), the American giant with major exposure to potash and phosphates. On a long-term basis, even if we include dividends (total return), we see that there has been no real shareholder value creation. The main added value seems to be inflation protection during periods when energy and crop prices soar.

And honestly, that works. Many make the case that we need to own some crops to protect against inflation. I disagree, as I think long-term investing in futures comes with unnecessary added costs like rollovers. Mosaic at least has a dividend of 3.5%. 2-3% exposure in a portfolio for added inflation protection makes a lot of sense in my book.

Other picks are Nutrien (NTR), which I sometimes call the “Canadian Mosaic,” as they often move in lockstep. Nutrien yields 2.9%.

Then there’s Deere & Company (DE), which is the only high-margin agriculture stock on my radar, as it sells high-end tractors and machinery with substantial pricing power. As its chart shows (it shows the ratio between Deere and the S&P 500), it has consistently generated shareholder value, making it a terrific dividend growth stock for most investors looking to buy some industrial exposure.

The downside is that Deere yields just 1.2%. Luckily, it has a 5-year dividend CAGR of 15.3% that far outpaces the dividend growth of higher-yielding industrial stocks.

I also want to bring up Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) here. This company is ultra critical to the global food supply chain, as it processes, transports, and distributes crops like corn and wheat, produces ethanol, food-related products, and manages silos, rail networks, and countless other assets that farmers and food companies rely on.

ADM yields slightly less than 3.0% and is looking at a rebound after three years of double-digit EPS contraction. In 2026 and 2027, analysts expect 20% and 19% EPS growth, respectively. I think it is able to beat these numbers if we get inflationary tailwinds. In general, I believe that due to the surge in energy prices and shifting demand to North American exports, this company could be a surprisingly good stock over the next few years.

Then there’s logistics.

• Logistics

Supply chains are worthless without efficient transportation. You cannot get fertilizers, energy, people, or anything else from Point A to Point B without reliable transportation. The Strait of Hormuz is a great example of that.

Here, I like all North American Class I railroads, as they all have wide moat business models, a focus on dividend growth, buybacks, and healthy balance sheets. However, two stand out today, which are Union Pacific (UNP) and the Canadian National Railway (CNI).

Union Pacific yields 2.3% with a track record of 19 consecutive annual dividend hikes. The company is in the process of merging with Norfolk Southern (NSC). While they are waiting for approval (which could get rejected), I am very upbeat, as even the president has made clear that he sees a strategic benefit in what could be the first transcontinental railroad in the U.S.

A strategic network like this would make it even easier to connect demand across the country and de-risk supply chains. For example, let’s say ports in the West struggle with capacity problems. Other ports could take over, as fast railroad connections could help with cross-country shipments. Especially in emergency situations, that’s valuable.

Canadian National yields 2.6% with a 5-year CAGR of 13.7%. It’s critical, as more than 65% of all traffic originates on its network, which connects 7 major ports across two nations. This network includes the corridor down to Louisiana, China (the biggest rail market), and both Canadian coasts.

These assets aren’t just critical, but they also have the ability to generate consistent shareholder value. Especially if I’m right about growth broadening, I think we’ll find alpha here.

Another honorable mention is Prologis (PLD). This is the world’s biggest industrial REIT with a focus on high-end warehouses that cater to some of the biggest consumer-focused companies on the planet, all of which need smooth value chains. This includes Amazon (AMZN). Currently, PLD yields 3.3% with a 5-year dividend CAGR of 11.6%.

As goods need warehouses and our modern economy relies on these supply chains, PLD is highly critical, and one of the few REITs I would literally call “mission-critical.”

• Chemicals

I’m almost at this point again, where I realize I am writing a way-too-long article. However, I need to mention Linde (LIN) and Air Products & Chemicals (APD), as two chemical giants that provide a number of critical products, including helium. Linde, for example, produces helium in places like Kansas and stores it in Texas. Especially in current times, we’re reminded just how important these companies are for our economies.

Linde yields 1.3% with a 5-year dividend CAGR of 9.1%. For APD, these numbers are 2.5% and 6.0%, respectively (higher yield, yet slower growth).

On a rather consistent basis, LIN has outperformed APD, as the LIN/APD total return ratio below shows. Going forward, I’m bullish on both, especially due to my cyclical value thesis.

I have to say that all of these companies are so fascinating that I could easily write 2,000+ words on each of them. And maybe, going forward, I will do just that.

But for now, here’s the takeaway:

Takeaway

On a rather frequent basis, wars remind us of how important (and fragile) global supply chains are. While I am biased due to my background that includes a supply chain-focused master’s degree, I am convinced that understanding supply chains is extremely critical.

Today, I gave you my take on some of the most critical companies, players that not only support our economies, but also have the ability to add tremendous value to portfolios and come with either decent income or consistent dividend growth.

None of these companies is likely to be replaced. They have no competition from AI, and, if I’m right about the rebound of the “cyclical economy,” I think the next few years will be very bullish for all of them.

So, stay tuned, as I’ll give these companies (and others) a lot more attention.

data.title: 2 ETFs I'm Dollar-Cost Averaging Into As The Case For A Bear Market Increases
data.description:
Introduction

In recent weeks, the Technology (XLK) sector has continued to slide where it entered into correction territory. In my opinion, this has a lot to do with the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.

While I expect more volatility in the near-term despite the Nasdaq (NDAQ) surging on potential news of the war ending soon, I'm continuing to dollar-cost average into some of my favorite growth ETFs.

Going forward, I remain bullish on artificial intelligence and anticipate growth-focused ETFs see a strong run after the conflict with Iran gets resolved. But in the short to medium-term, these funds could see muted returns.

In this article, I discuss the two ETFs I'm buying currently, and why both are attractive if you're focused on growth and have a long-term time horizon.

Tech Stocks Headed For A Bear Market?

Coming into 2026, the broader market had solid momentum after closing up more than 16% in 2025. Rate cut hopes were on the table. But now with the ongoing war and President Trump threatening to blow up Iran's oil wells if they fail to make a deal, we could see a bear market soon.

Whether the two sides can come to an agreement remains to be seen, but investors are growing worried as the days go on. Currently, it appears many are raising cash. And while the market seems to be in oversold territory, I think we see a steeper sell-off if we do end up continuing operations in Iran.

The main reason is because we are likely to see higher inflation. Coming into the new year, the market was pricing in interest rate cuts. Now, there's a potential to see rate hikes. If we do see a hike, I believe this will send the market into bear territory. But of course, a lot can change in a short amount of time.

My suggestion is investors have some cash readily available to take advantage of the buying opportunities I anticipate in the near to medium term. While I'm currently buying the two ETFs I discuss today in small batches, I would load up should we see a bear market.

#1 Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG)

First ETF I'm buying is SCHG. As a result of the Technology sector entering into correction territory, the ETF has underperformed year-to-date. SCHG is down double-digits compared to the S&P (SP500), who's down roughly 7%.

SCHG has 45% exposure to the Technology sector with behemoths like NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Broadcom (AVGO), Alphabet (GOOGL), META Platforms (META), and Amazon (AMZN) making up its top 10 holdings.

But as you can see from the chart below, all have underperformed significantly year-to-date, down double-digits at the time of writing. In my opinion, this has nothing to do with the companies' fundamentals but more to do with the strong performances they saw in the years prior.

When stocks see a strong rally, they're likely to see periods of underperformance afterward. As an investor, focusing on the fundamentals and long-term performance is key.

While past performance is no indicator of future performance, SCHG's long-term track record speaks for itself. And this is why it's prudent to zoom out. Since its inception on December 11, 2009, SCHG has outperformed the largest S&P 500 ETF, (VOO) in total returns, led by its higher exposure to Technology.

Here's a break down of the fund's annualized performance since 2015 until 2024. As you can see below, 2023 was their strongest year so far with over 50% returns. In 2025, SCHG closed up 17.63% compared to 16.65% for the S&P.

I'm also a fan of SCHG's more concentrated portfolio with 196 holdings compared to VOO's over 500. While the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) outperformed SCHG pretty significantly, SCHG's better price point and lower expense ratio are reasons I prefer the ETF.

Going forward, I think AI will continue to shape the economic landscape. In the near term, AI stocks could see volatility as fears of labor market disruption could weigh on the performance of tech stocks and funds alike.

But long-term, I believe the rapid adoption of AI will benefit SCHG and peers alike. And as a result, SCHG will likely see strong returns.

#2 Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV)

As the name implies, CGDV provides investors with income. But something I like about CGDV is although the fund focuses on dividends, it acts like a growth fund.

This is because the ETF focuses on value investing. CGDV looks for undervalued stocks based in the U.S. who have the potential to see strong capital appreciation long-term.

Probably my favorite thing about CGDV is their multi-manager approach. The ETF currently has three portfolio managers, each focusing on a specific sleeve of the portfolio.

In my opinion, this gives them a higher chance of outperforming long-term despite their shorter track record. CGDV has an inception date of June 24, 2025. So, quite different from SCHG.

SCHG is passively-managed, which is why their expense ratio is low at just 0.04%. CGDV is also reasonable at 0.33%, solid for an actively-managed fund.

Another key difference is SCHG undergoes a full reconstitution annually. CGDV's managers offload stocks whenever they see fit because of its actively-managed, multi-manager strategy.

CGDV has decent exposure to the Technology sector for a dividend-focused fund. Currently, the fund has 12.6% exposure. Their top sector is the Industrial (XLI) sector followed by Financials (XLF), Technology, and then Healthcare (XLV).

I believe CGDV's current exposure give them a perfect blend of growth and income as well as downside protection should we see a prolonged bear market. This is because of the exposure to Healthcare, which accounts for 11.2% of the portfolio.

Below are their current top 10 holdings:

Year-to-date, CGDV has experienced less of a drawdown compared to other popular ETFs. SCHG has seen the largest decline, driven by its high exposure to Technology stocks.

SCHG's sister fund, the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) has performed well, led by its exposure to the Energy (XLE) sector. While capital appreciation is important for a lot of investors, seeing less of a pullback is also something to pay close attention to as this can speak to a fund's quality.

Since inception, CGDV managed to outperform VOO and SCHG in total returns despite their higher exposure to tech. This speaks to their quality despite having only 9 months public.

Why These Two ETFs

In my opinion, SCHG & CGDV give investors the perfect blend of growth and income. Furthermore, both take different approaches with SCHG's passively-managed style and CGDV's multi-manager, active approach to value investing.

Both are core positions in my portfolio and offer manageable overlap at 31%. SCHG gives me the largest exposure to stocks poised to benefit from AI. They also offer a low-cost structure and a proven long-term track record.

CGDV's track record is significantly shorter. But I believe their active approach with having multiple portfolio managers gives them an edge over competitors.

Their capital appreciation first approach also make them the perfect ETF to hold as a core holding and build individual income positions around.

Both SCHG & CGDV also pay tax-favorable distributions despite having lower yields. This makes them desirable for holding inside taxable accounts.

But as previously mentioned, both funds could see underperformance risks despite me being bullish on the future of AI and strong past performances. Particularly, if we do end up deploying ground troops, which is think at the moment is unlikely.

But long-term, I think the brief correction in tech stocks make them even more attractive. So, if you're looking for growth-focused ETFs that have the potential to make great long term core holdings for a growth-centric portfolio, then consider both SCHG and CGDV.

data.title: Misunderstood 10-13% Yields For Important Retirement Portfolio Diversification
data.description:
One of the big flaws of income investing is that it often ends up pigeonholing investors into a select few sectors, thereby leaving them without adequate diversification. For example, midstream, real estate (VNQ), utilities (XLU), and other infrastructure sectors (UTF), as well as BDCs (BIZD) and other debt sectors (HYG), often make up the vast majority of their portfolios.

They may also choose to invest in dividend growth ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). However, these funds can still leave them with relatively low exposure to some key sectors like precious metals (GLD) and mega cap tech (QQQ). I name these two sectors in particular because they are among some of the best performing sectors in recent years.

The reason they get left out is that gold by itself does not pay dividends, and gold mining stocks (GDX) tend to not pay much in the way of a high yield, while mega cap tech pays between little and no dividends either. Therefore, they often get skipped.

Two Funds That Bridge the Gap

However, there are some funds that can provide exposure to these key sectors along with income while still trying to preserve some of the upside potential, making them often misunderstood vehicles that can provide valuable portfolio diversification for income investors. This article will detail two of them, namely the NEOS Gold High-Income ETF (IAUI) and the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ).

Both of these funds are valuable diversifiers not only because they provide underlying exposure to gold and mega cap tech while generating attractive income through their notional covered call strategies, but also because they have dynamically managed options coverage of their underlying long exposure to gold and mega cap tech, varying the amount based on market conditions and fundamentals at the manager's discretion. This thereby enables them to preserve some upside potential while also balancing it with generating attractive income.

Gold Income With Tax Efficiency

IAUI has not been around for very long, as it was launched in early June of last year, but since that time it has already made a name for itself by winning the Best New Options Income ETF at the 2026 ETF.com Awards and growing its AUM into the hundreds of millions.

In particular, it is popular because it generates monthly distributions with an annualized yield of around 13% based on its monthly payout rate. It has also delivered attractive total returns since its launch, albeit lower than its underlying gold focus (IAU):

One of the big downsides of the fund, beyond its underperformance relative to gold, is that it charges a fairly high expense ratio of 0.78%, but at least you get an option strategy with this and semi-active management of the fund.

IAUI is structured to have up to 25% of its holdings in gold ETPs like SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) and then up to 75% in synthetic long gold positions via a long call plus a short put position. It then sells calls on top of that with 50 to 100% notional coverage to generate its monthly income. It also uses flex options with monthly expirations while actively rolling them based on managed discretion, including on the strike levels and the coverage percentage.

As a result, the vast majority of its distributions have been estimated as return of capital in the past, which makes it very tax efficient for long-term investors. This is because it means that a lot of the income does not get taxed as income or even long-term capital gains. Once the cost basis hits zero from distributions, the gains beyond that are taxed at long-term capital gains rate, making IAUI a very tax-efficient way to generate income from gold.

Could Sideways Gold Be IAUI's Sweet Spot?

In addition to the tax efficiency, this fund generates consistently high income and can help investors do well even in environments where gold is trading sideways, given gold's recent strong run higher yet still favorable tailwinds, such as the de-dollarization trend and strong central bank demand. This could be a good time to have IAUI, as market wariness of its elevated valuation versus structural tailwinds could lead to some sideways trading in gold markets, in which case IAUI will likely outperform the underlying metal. This has proven true over the past few weeks where gold has largely traded sideways and IAUI has outperformed it:

Of course, the main trade-offs are the higher expense ratio, the capped upside whenever gold makes a strong move higher, which tends to be quite common for gold during its bull markets, while also not protecting much against a gold bear market, since it generates options premium but does not actually explicitly hedge against the downside.

Mega Cap Tech Income at Scale

Meanwhile, GPIQ is a few years older than IAUI, since it was launched in October 2023, and since then has grown its AUM to above $3 billion. Like IAUI, it generates income monthly from a covered call-type strategy, paying that out to unit holders via monthly distributions. Its trailing 12-month yield sits at around 10%, and it has one of the most attractive expense ratios in the covered call space, with a 0.29% expense ratio that just recently adjusted to 0.35% this month.

GPIQ has about half of its portfolio invested in tech stocks (XLK), with the vast majority of that being in mega cap tech stocks that do not generate much in the way of income themselves. It also implements a dynamic call overwrite strategy between 25% and 75% of the portfolio, which preserves upside potential during strong bull markets, though it still does cap that to some degree. Its strategy has proven successful over time, as it has outperformed several of its peer covered call funds that focus on tech stocks.

Not only that, but its use of Section 1256 contracts means that its distributions are taxed at blended long-term and short-term rates, versus ordinary income treatment for ELNs from funds like the J.P. Morgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ). Additionally, it has a much lower expense ratio than peers like the NXF Nasdaq 100 Income ETF (QQQI), which charges a 0.68% expense ratio, which has helped to fuel its outperformance over time.

Why Retirees Should Take a Closer Look

Both these funds are misunderstood because they provide valuable underlying diversification to two very important sectors, along with attractive income, but at the same time do not fully give up the upside potential that many of their covered call peers do. Thus they can be valuable components for a portfolio. At the same time, the capped upside and the higher expense ratios relative to simply holding the underlying assets via ETFs like Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) and SPDR Gold MiniShares Trust (GLDM), or even better yet, in bullion's case, owning bullion leases that combine attractive yields with full ownership of the underlying bullion and no expense ratios, are real trade-offs to consider.

For investors who need high yields and want exposure to these sectors for diversification, they can be very valuable positions, especially in the case of retirees who may want to take a bit more of a passive approach to managing their investments and want diversified funds that are actively managed. Thus, while they are not necessarily a good fit for my portfolios at High Yield Investor, since there we are focused on primarily picking individual stocks, bonds, and preferreds to maximize income and total returns, which we've been able to do very well so far, IAUI and GPIQ could be good fits for certain types of retirees.

data.title: Box: Valuation Is Still Too Cheap At This Level
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on BOX, Inc. (BOX) in Dec '25, which I recommended a buy rating due to my expectation that revenue growth will continue to accelerate as suggested by the forward indicators, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock.

I remain bullish on BOX. Enterprise Advanced is becoming a real growth driver, the product roadmap is getting stronger, and the go-to-market motion is proving to be effective. The valuation also still looks too cheap for a business that, in my view, has a credible path to mid-teens growth.

Enterprise Advanced

In December, my bull case for BOX was really based on one idea, and that is growth should accelerate as the gap between recognized revenue and the forward indicators closes. I still believe that. But that is no longer the whole story for BOX, as Enterprise Advanced has become a real revenue growth driver. To refresh readers’ memory, in Q4, management said Enterprise Advanced reached 10% of revenue just one year after launch, and they also said it is carrying a 30% to 40% price uplift vs. Enterprise Plus. One point to note here is that enterprise advanced growth did not come at the expense of enterprise plus. Overall suites (Advanced + Plus) are now 66% of revenue, up from 60% in Q4 2025. This mix shift tells us that BOX is successfully pushing more of its base into a better product mix with better monetization.

I have high confidence that we will continue to see adoption pick up as these are in demand for real use cases. I point readers back to the latest Q4 transcript, where management explicitly cited more use cases. I previously touched on data extraction but am positively surprised by new use cases being brought up like quote creation and approvals and even contractual risk management. Those are practical workflow problems that drag down labor productivity. As I said before, the idea is to save employees time by cutting manual work, and since there is strong visibility to return-on-investment [ROI] here, it is easy to justify in a budget meeting (for the client).

There are a couple of upcoming product launches that also add additional layers of confidence. As I noted before, Box Extract is the key part, and BOX is going to roll out more complex extraction methods. Now that is good, but the upcoming product, BOX Automate, set to launch in 1H 2027, is going to make this employee’s life even better. Automation basically takes the extracted data and moves it through a real business process. For that to work better, BOX is going to make it easier to build workflows using apps (think of things like dashboards, intake forms, or contract review tools) without needing heavy coding. In essence, these new products should drive up broader adoption because they further improve productivity (the idea is that after adoptng extraction, why not automation too?).

Another important part here is the platform layer. The reality is most customers are not going to use just one AI model. It is most likely going to be using Claude for one task, Copilot for another, ChatGPT for another, etc. To ensure that BOX benefits from all of such usage, the ideal position is to sit underneath all of these platforms, and I think it makes sense. BOX does not need to win the entire AI application layer; it just needs to be the trusted system where clients' content lives and where the work gets routed. If customers connect more outside tools and agents into BOX, that should create another monetization path through API calls and AI unit consumption, not just seat growth.

Awareness

Not only is the product side of things doing well, the go-to-market side of things also looks much better. Previously, I had a good sense about the demand mainly from tracking the billings, RPO (remaining performance obligations), and NRR (net retention rate). The good thing is there are now more datapoints for us to get a better sense of things. At the analyst day, management said awareness went up 4x y/y in FY2026 and also the go-to-market strategy is now focused on use-case-led selling, outreach beyond CIOs into CISOs (chief information security officers) and other IT decision-makers, and better use of partners and developers. In simpler terms, BOX is going hard on expanding its top-of-funnel presence, and the idea is the more awareness BOX creates (which is working), the more leads flow through the funnel, which naturally should lead to more conversions.

Valuation

The valuation today is simply too attractive to ignore. I have made my case on the fundamental size of things above, which makes the growth acceleration to mid-teens plausible. On the valuation side of things, at the current 3x forward revenue, we are not really paying a lot. In the base case, even if multiples were to stay at 3x, I can still bridge to >30% upside as you see above. Note that 3x is the -2 standard deviation level. And if we go back to 3.5x, which is the -1 standard deviation level, the upside is even more attractive. Note that I am not even suggesting we get back to the mean, which makes more sense to me since BOX has historically traded at 4x when growth was ~10%. I also compare BOX to Dropbox (DBX), which has a 0% growth outlook but trades at the same multiple as BOX, which doesn’t make sense.

As for my revenue growth assumption, I am assuming BOX can grow into the mid-teens over time as Enterprise Advanced continues to scale, more customers adopt higher-value workflow products like Extract and Automate, and the company adds incremental monetization through APIs and AI-unit consumption. The stronger go-to-market motion should also support both expansion within the installed base and new customer conversion.

Risk

One risk I like to point out is that BOX may be entering a heavier investment phase before the newer revenue streams become meaningful. Management is spending more on go-to-market and product expansion, but platform revenue and AI-unit monetization are still small today. If these newer revenue streams take longer to scale, BOX could end up with weaker margins in the near term, and that could weigh on the stock sentiment.

Conclusion

I reiterate a buy rating on BOX. My view is stronger than before, as revenue growth still looks set to accelerate, it has a better product mix, more awareness is built, and there are more monetization paths through the platform layer. Moreover, the stock still trades at a level that does not reflect this setup.

data.title: U.S. Manufacturers Show Resilience On Outbreak Of War Despite Rising Prices And Supply Delays
data.description:
US manufacturers reported faster growth of output and order books in March, pointing to encouraging resilience in the face of the outbreak of war in the Middle East. However, key areas of concern at the moment are prices and supply chains. A sharp rise in prices and delivery delays has cast a cloud over the outlook, threatening to drive inflation higher, dampen demand and throttle supply chains.

Robust output growth persists

S&P Global’s manufacturing PMI surveys indicated rising output among manufacturers in March, the rate of growth ticking higher to round off a solid first quarter. Production has now risen for eight straight months, with the March PMI reading consistent with a monthly rise in industrial production of approximately 0.5%.

Demand was reportedly supported principally by improved domestic sales, in part linked to protection from tariffs and some inventory building, offsetting a further drop in exports. Export sales have dropped continually over the past nine months, but total order book volumes have risen in all of the past 15 months bar December, rising at an increased rate in March.

Price and supply worries

Few instances were reported of the war in the Middle East having so far directly affecting either production or order books, although March saw some cases of manufacturers and their customers building stocks as a precaution against future price rises or supply shortages.

Prices paid for inputs have jumped higher thanks primarily to the impact of higher oil prices, with the rate of inflation accelerating to the highest since last August, while supplier delays have become more widely reported than at any time since October 2022, linked to the war exacerbating existing shipping, haulage and port delays.

Hiring on hold

March also saw a worrying lack of hiring, with manufacturing payrolls instead remaining under pressure as factories sought to reduce staffing costs in the face of rising energy and raw material prices. Only a marginal increase in employment was reported by the survey panel, representing the smallest increase since last July and suggestive of a further fall in official manufacturing payroll numbers according to historical comparisons.

Outlook steady, for now

If price pressures and supply delays persist, demand, employment and production capabilities will inevitably start to be more seriously affected. However, business confidence regarding output in the year ahead has so far held up well, especially in relation to manufacturers in other economies, notably in Europe. Expectations for output in the year ahead cooled only marginally in March among US producers, running at one of the highest levels seen over the past year.

Sustained optimism, in part reflecting reduced concerns over government policies such as tariffs, but also indicated that producers anticipate only a short-term and modest impact from the war. Much, therefore, hinges on the duration of the war and its related energy market disruption.

There may also be a delay by which the rise in energy prices (and related pull-back in interest rate cut expectations) feeds through to order books, as customers start to feel the pinch more in April. Supply chain delays will also take a while to feed through. Upcoming PMI data will therefore be important to monitor.

© 2026, S&P Global. All rights reserved. Reproduction in whole or in part without permission is prohibited.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Okeanis Eco Tankers: Stronger Fundamentals, But Now A Geopolitical Trade
data.description:
When I first wrote an article about Okeanis Eco Tankers (ECO), the thesis was quite simple, especially if we compare it to today. It was and still is a modern tanker operator, which I consider to be one of the most effective in the sector. The company at the time had new VLCC and Suezmax fleet, which allowed them to generate around 35% EBIT margin and around 22% net profit margin. Many of it's competitors never reached these kind of numbers. Also, ROE was around 19% which immediately put it on my screener and was almost double the sectors average. At the time of my article it was a good cyclical buy, as it was high quality stock which was not priced in yet.

Since then, the situation has changed quite a lot. The stock went from $25 to around $50 level. It is now quite obvious that part of the upside is already realised. The market started valuing this business and the sentiment become more bullish on shipping stocks. This is apparent especially now when the tanker rates are high and the companies results are better than expected. As of now, the company is not a play for me anymore, yet I believe it has some upside potential. I like stocks that are overlooked, which this company is not anymore, ECO is now more on the radar.

The most important change however is not in the price, but in overall setup. When I wrote an article it was cheap cyclical bet with clear upside logic. Now, I believe it is more of a geopolitics play. Why? Because earnings are now dependent more on events in Middle East region than from cycle. In other words, ECO is now more of a geopolitics driven play than a tanker company play.

Business

If we look at the results themselves, the company is in better condition since my first article. Q4 2025 was a strong quarter, which mainly was driven by higher tanker rates. TCE earnings increased to $76 thousand a day, which is completely different regime than a year ago. It is important to understand, that this a spot market effect and we cannot trust it that the results will continue to be that good. Given that major part of the fleet is in the spot market, they can catch the rates fast and it can immediately be seen in profits.

Balance is also a little bit better, but it is not clean. The cash increased to almost $120 million, which gives flexibility. But at the same time ECO remains leveraged with significant debt level. It is common in this sector to be fair. By the end of 2025 the company initiated equity issuance ($130 million) to finance their fleet expansion. And this is the part I do not like - shareholder dilution. In short, we see better balance sheet, but part of this greatness comes not from operations, but from new capital.

Their fleet also changing and quite well. The company is moving from 14 ships to bigger fleet, and has now 16 tankers, and plans additional Suezmax newbuilds. From what I gathered we should see new vessels in 2026. This in turn means better earning capacity in the future. It is good that the company has modern, emissions regulation compliant ships that I consider a competitive advantage of theirs.

However, I am as always very sceptical. The bigger the fleet, means the bigger spot exposure, which means earning leverage. When the rates will go up, the profits will go up too. Tricky part is that it works in the opposite way the same. With better fleet, the company becomes higher beta play on tanker market.

War Impact

The conflict in Middle East is not really good for tanker sector as we already know. It affects one of the main global oil supply points. Some of the readers will be already informed, some might not, but Strait of Hormuz is considered one of the most important chokepoints in the world. One fifth of the global oil trade goes through this point. As of now, the risk is huge, we see stocks collapsing, then going back up and back down. Any problems in the region I mentioned tend to spill over from oil price to physical transportation.

I do not want to get political, but I will have to state the obvious. The oil price is not really the point here. When Middle East flows become less reliable and more complicated. Buyers, especially from Asia start to look for alternatives. And we have players like U.S., Brasil, West Africa. The problem is that these regions are quite far. Reality is that the longer the trip, the more time it takes to deliver the cargo and fleets become less effective. In practice, I have noticed historically that just means less available fleet in the market and pressure on spot tariffs.

I might sound unethical, but why is it good for this company? The majority of the fleet is in the spot market, which means that they can immediately start monetising changes like that. Tariffs are going up due to ton-mile inflation and regional problems. ECO earnings should reflect that because due to spot market exposure it moves with the market without huge lag. While, it is my opinion and my opinion only, I believe that the war or situation will change in the region any time soon, the company becomes a good play. Geopolitical shocks like that sometimes generate disproportionately high profits for spot oriented operators.

The risks

This situation that we are currently in is a clear tailwind for the tanker market. But it is very important to understand that this is not just "higher rates = good". The conflict poses risks to operators and to be fair, some of them might start avoiding certain routes, which creates route instability. What it means is that the trips will be longer, but also very ineffective. Another risk that comes into picture is insurance. The ships are like cars, they need to be insured and whenever the risk increases, insurance costs can start putting pressure on profitability. Crew safety regulations tend to change too and sometimes companies are forced to change routes or even stay at the port waiting for safer windows to pass through.

The biggest question for me is now earnings stability for ECO. Their model as we talked is oriented to spot market, which at this time is very good. I feel it is a double-edged sword though. The same mechanism that increases earnings can quickly push it down. If rates start normalising, the revenue might fall as quick as it was going up. Without long-term contracts it is just a dependency on geopolitics as of now. War premium will not be here forever.

Finally, I think it is worth noting that dividends are not guaranteed at all. As of now, it looks attractive and the company is growing, so it might look like a good opportunity. Reality is that the current situation will have to ease off or end one day, meaning that the premium company gets on rates will go down too. Payout comes from the cycle. While the rates are up, dividends look very good. But when the cycle turns, it might be the first thing the company will reduce.

Valuation

As I mentioned, the company is not as cheap as it was in my first article. Looking at forward P/E, ECO is already traded at a premium compared to it's peers. It is even almost at the same level as a mature tanker operators. At the same time we have EV/EBITDA which still looks competitive, but understand that this metric stands on higher earnings cycle point.

We should be looking at balance sheet multiples also. P/B is around 3.15, which is way higher than most of the peers. This shows, that the market has already priced in not only current profitability, but expects it to have the same earnings profile in the future. The problem is that in the tankers sector expectations are rarely stable. Current valuation is only justifiable if the rates remain high (which I believe will). If the cycle goes back to normal, these multiples can contract fast.

Summary

The company today is stronger than at the time I analysed it. The results are better, fleet is expanding, and spot exposure gives opportunity for better profitability. But at the same time, the investment logic changed. It is not undervalued cyclical play anymore, now it is more of a geopolitical bet. The earnings I believe are depending on current market regime. I will be maintaining my rating as a buy, the company is now good for tactical trades, not as a long-term investment.

data.title: Best Buy: Despite Sales Headwinds, Dividend And Low P/E Are Tough To Ignore (Upgrade)
data.description:
2026 has been the year of rather indiscriminate selling in the stock market. Investors have latched onto negative macro headlines, ranging from skyrocketing oil prices and the escalating war in Iran, to a weak consumer economy and the possibility for AI disruption. While we can hardly say that the economy and corporate earnings are on strong footing, we also think the selloff in many stocks has gone too far: and created tremendous buying opportunities in several names.

Best Buy (BBY), the largest pure-play electronics retailer in the U.S., has been dealing with weak comp sales all across last year; that said, the company has maintained market share as the industry declined. The stock, however, is in bear market territory after plunging ~25% from peaks above $84 notched last October, and since January alone the stock has given up a further ~10%.

I last wrote a neutral article on Best Buy in November, when the stock was still trading at $80 per share. Since then, the massive erosion in Best Buy's share price, plus the company's recent dividend increase, have substantially increased Best Buy's appeal for me as an income strategy. While I think that we should certainly keep an eye on weakening recent sales trends, I'm not hesitating to use this juncture as a buy-the-dip opportunity. I'm raising my rating on Best Buy to buy.

Even on flat sales, Best Buy can support its raised dividend

Let's first kick off the discussion with the most attractive piece of Best Buy's narrative today: its dividend yield. Alongside its Q4 earnings print, Best Buy announced a 1% increase in its dividend to $0.96 per share. On an annualized basis, this is $3.84, which at Best Buy's current share prices near $64 translates to a dividend yield of 6.0%.

Best Buy has always been an attractive income play; now, with yields pushing 6% (a huge premium over falling Treasury yields), this stock has become a much more viable income strategy. That said, the core question is: can Best Buy really fund this dividend, given the steep decline in its business?

We find that it can. The chart below showcases Best Buy's cash flow in FY26. Last year, the company generated $1.96 billion in free cash flow, while spending $704 million on capex, leading to $1.26 billion in free cash flow. Meanwhile, the company spent $801 million on dividends and $273 million in share buybacks.

This means the company's dividend payout ratio was a still-healthy 64%; inclusive of buybacks, payout ratio across all shareholder returns was still reasonable at 85%.

Let's now zoom forward to FY27, which is the current year for Best Buy ending in January 2027. For this year, the company is expecting comp sales to be flat (-1% to +1% range), with revenue of $41.2-$42.1 billion also representing ~0% growth at the midpoint.

Importantly, however, Best Buy is still targeting earnings growth this year. It's expecting adjusted operating income of 4.3-4.4%, which is 100bps higher than 3.3% in FY25. The company notes here that it's baking in a 30bps expected increase to full-year gross margins (thanks to higher contributions from ads and Best Buy Marketplace, the company's third-party marketplace business in which it earns commission revenue).

All told, flat revenue and a slightly richer margin profile should lead to improved free cash flow in FY27 (even if Best Buy steps up its capex from $704 million in FY26 to $750 million in FY27).

Comps weakness is targeted to certain categories, and Best Buy is still maintaining share

Of course, we can't ignore the core risk that Best Buy faces: the recent downturn in sales trends. In Q4, the company generated $13.81 billion in sales, down -1% y/y and missing Wall Street's exceptions of $13.88 billion (closer to flat y/y) by a one-point margin.

Comp sales, meanwhile (which measures the revenue growth from existing stores, and excludes the contribution of newly opened or recent closed stores) fell at -0.8% y/y domestically, and an even sharper -1.3% overseas.

There is good news here, however. Weakness has largely been confined to a few categories. Appliances, consumer electronics, and entertainment (which together represent just shy of 50% of the company's overall revenue) are the major decliners; that said, Best Buy is still enjoying voracious demand for mobile phones and computers, which achieved mid single digit comp sales growth in the U.S.

There's certainly cyclicality in buying patterns, especially for large items like appliances (and we're still facing headwinds from a weak housing market as well, which is usually the impetus for buying large appliances like dishwashers) . We note, importantly, that Best Buy believes it has at least maintained (if not grown) market share, and the company also notes that sales trends improved across December and January. Its guidance for Q1 calls for a return to positive +1% comps growth. Per CEO Corie Barry's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Our Q4 comparable sales were down 0.8% versus last year, within our guidance range for the quarter. Our data sources show our market share was at least flat, pointing to slightly softer consumer demand for our industry during the holiday quarter. Our holiday customer demand patterns were also different than modeled, despite sales event timing that was very similar to last year's. We saw softer-than-expected sales in November and the beginning of December. We then experienced strong sales in the last 2 weeks of December and the start of January, and sales were negatively impacted by weather-induced store closures during the last week of the quarter.

We were prepared for a promotional holiday and the environment was even a bit more promotional than we factored heading into the quarter."

Again, we think pessimism is already baked into the stock, and we like that even at a flat sales profile, the company can still readily support its increased dividend.

The GameStop variable; risks and key takeaways

There's one final variable that's worth mentioning: recently, speculation has increased that GameStop (GME) may be looking to acquire Best Buy outright. GameStop's Ryan Cohen has mentioned that he is looking to make a transformational acquisition to turn GameStop around.

This scenario has become, in my view, far more likely with the recent buildup in GameStop's balance sheet and the erosion in Best Buy's market value. See the snapshot below: as of the end of Q4, GameStop carried $9.0 billion in cash (not to mention $368 million in Bitcoin that is marked to market; though Gamestop gets a lot of attention for its crypto holdings, its cash balances are actually far more vast).

At current share prices near $63, Best Buy has a market cap of $13.5 billion, and a lower enterprise value of $12.9 billion (the company has $1.74 billion in cash offset against $1.17 billion in debt, or a ~$0.57 billion net cash position). Given both GameStop and Best Buy's clean balance sheet, the deal looks feasible with only a modest amount of debt thrown in on top of GameStop's balance sheet.

Of course, there are risks we need to keep in mind here. It's possible that Best Buy's stock today is being propped up, at least slightly, by hopes of an acquisition - and if GameStop announces an alternative target, Best Buy shares could falter. We should also keep an eye on weakening comp sales trends and a tepid consumer economy, as Best Buy's guidance for FY27 relies on at least flat comps (which would be an improvement to Q4's declines).

That said, we find risks to be priced into the stock, with Best Buy trading at just a 10.0x FY27 P/E (based on the midpoint of its $6.30-$6.60 pro forma EPS guidance for this year) and yielding a healthy ~6.0% to reward investors for patience.

Use the dip here as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Commodities: Oil Jumps Amid Trump's Escalation Warning
data.description:
By Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist | Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy

Energy – Oil jumps after Trump’s war escalation threats

Oil rebounded sharply on Thursday, rising more than 5% after two days of declines, as US President Donald Trump threatened a further escalation of the war with Iran, injecting fresh uncertainty into energy markets.

Brent was trading above $107/bbl, while WTI was close to $106/bbl on Thursday morning following Trump’s address to the nation last night. The President said the US would hit Iran “extremely hard” over the next two to three weeks and could target “each and every one” of the country’s power plants. He also claimed the Strait of Hormuz would reopen “naturally” once the conflict ends, though he offered no details or a clear timeline. Trump additionally urged allies reliant on Middle Eastern energy to help resolve the near‑closure of Hormuz.

Even if shipping through the Strait of Hormuz resumes, a return to prewar market conditions is likely to be slow, as upstream production restarts, logistics normalisation and inventory rebuilding will take time.

US inventory data were mixed. EIA figures showed crude stocks rose for a sixth consecutive week, increasing by 5.5m barrels, below the 10.3m‑barrel build reported earlier by the API. Total crude inventories climbed to 461.6m barrels, the highest since June 2023 and close to the five‑year average. Cushing stocks rose by 520k barrels to 31.5m barrels, also the highest since July 2024. Crude imports were steady at 6.5m b/d, while exports increased by 199k b/d to 3.5m b/d.

In refined products, gasoline and distillate stocks fell by 0.6m barrels and 2.1m barrels, respectively, reflecting weak refinery runs despite rising implied demand amid heightened supply concerns. Refinery utilisation slipped 0.8ppt week-on-week to 92.1%.

Metals – Aluminium rallies on more Gulf smelter halts

Aluminium prices rallied above $3,500/t on Thursday after reports that Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) halted operations at its Al Taweelah smelter after the site was hit by Iranian missiles and drones over the weekend, according to Wood Mackenzie. According to the report, utilisation at Alba’s smelter has fallen to around 30%, from 81% previously. For now, these reports have not been confirmed by the companies.

If confirmed, a halt at EGA’s 1.6 mtpa Al Taweelah smelter, Alba’s reduced operations and earlier curtailments at Qatalum would take around 3 mtpa of capacity offline – close to half of Middle East aluminium production. That marks a sharp escalation from the roughly 8-9% disruption we were flagging in mid‑March.

This would imply materially deeper aluminium deficits across all our scenarios, lifting the severe disruption case to around 2-2.5 Mt, with even the base case now pointing to a deficit of roughly 1 Mt.

With Chinese supply capped and Indonesia’s ramping up constrained by power and limited capacity growth elsewhere, aluminium’s fundamentals remain firmly supportive. Prolonged Middle East disruptions add further tightening risk.

EGA has also reportedly moved to sell large volumes of alumina in the wake of the strike, according to a Bloomberg report.

LME aluminium prices are up around 17% year‑to‑date, with the forward curve in steep backwardation. That said, escalating global growth concerns could still lead to some demand destruction, partially offsetting the supply shock.

Aluminium, along with other base metals, pared earlier gains on Thursday morning after President Trump reiterated threats to attack Iran’s power plants, with rising rate risks renewing concerns over demand destruction.

Agriculture – Sugar prices ease on weaker demand signals

Sugar prices fell for a fourth consecutive session, briefly dipping towards US¢15/lb (-3% DoD), as softer oil prices reduced incentives for mills to divert output towards ethanol. While the front‑month contract is still up almost 12% over the past month – its strongest run since 2023, supported by crude’s earlier rally on Middle East tensions – recent hopes of de‑escalation have weighed on energy markets and, in turn, on sugar. Elevated volatility and uncertainty around the timing of any resolution have also encouraged position‑trimming. Meanwhile, favourable weather conditions in Brazil continue to support cane development, reinforcing expectations of ample supply in the upcoming season.

In India, data from the National Federation of Cooperative Sugar Factories show 2025/26 sugar production up 9% year-on-year to 27.1mt as of 31 March. The number of operating mills fell to 74, from 113 a year earlier, while cumulative cane crushing rose to 283.5mt, up from 265.5mt over the same period.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
